serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Bang Bang Chicken is approximately 150 g, typically presented as a plate of cold shredded chicken over julienned cucumber, drizzled with sesame-peanut-chili sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, roasted (NDB 05013) adapted with sesame-peanut sauce components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry for Bang Bang Chicken exists. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken breast (NDB 05013) with proportional contributions from sesame paste, peanut butter, soy sauce, and cucumber based on a standard recipe ratio. The input record values (165 kcal, 14.5 g protein, 10.2 g fat, 4.5 g carbs per 100 g) are consistent with this USDA-derived estimate and are retained. Saturated fat estimated at 2.2 g per 100 g based on USDA fatty acid profiles for chicken and sesame/peanut components. The original input source (Sichuan University of Science and Engineering) is not on the approved source list and has been replaced.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked chicken dish with sesame-peanut sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bang Bang Chicken. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked chicken dishes with nut-based sauces. The dish derives most of its energy from protein and fat; carbohydrate content is 4.5 g per 100 g (6.75 g per 150 g serving). Glycemic load per serving = 25 × 6.75 / 100 = 1.69, rounded to 1.7 (very low).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce (380 mg per 100 g; ~570 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-soy-sauce preparation, as a standard 150 g serving contributes approximately 570 mg sodium, representing roughly 25% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by clinical guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (4.5 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (estimated GI 25)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bang Bang Chicken is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate load (approximately 6.75 g per 150 g serving) and high protein content (21.75 g per serving), which supports glycaemic control and satiety; however, the sesame-peanut sauce adds caloric density and should be portioned mindfully.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (10.2 g per 100 g) with beneficial unsaturated fat profile from sesame and peanut, offset by elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The unsaturated fat profile from sesame paste and peanut butter is cardioprotective, but the elevated sodium content (380 mg per 100 g) warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; preparation with reduced soy sauce and portion control is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bang Bang Chicken (棒棒鸡 / 棒棒雞, Bàngbàng Jī) is a classic cold dish from Sichuan cuisine in Southwest China. The name derives from the wooden stick (bang) traditionally used to pound poached chicken, tenderising the meat and loosening the fibres so it can be shredded by hand into fine, silky strands. The dish is served cold, typically arranged over a bed of julienned cucumber, and finished with a rich, complex sauce made from sesame paste, peanut butter, light soy sauce, black vinegar, sugar, and Sichuan chili oil, garnished with toasted sesame seeds and spring onion. The flavour profile is a celebrated Sichuan harmony of creamy, nutty, savoury, sweet, and spicy notes, with the signature mala (numbing-spicy) sensation from Sichuan peppercorns. Nutritionally, Bang Bang Chicken is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish providing approximately 165 kcal per 100 g, with 14.5 g protein, 10.2 g fat, and only 4.5 g carbohydrate. The fat profile is predominantly unsaturated, contributed by sesame and peanut components. Sodium is elevated at 380 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce. The dish has a low estimated glycaemic index (~25) and is clinically suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management. It is commonly served as an appetiser, cold side dish, or light main course at lunch or dinner, and is popular in both traditional Sichuan restaurants and modern Chinese cuisine globally. Note: the input record carried the erroneous name 'Chicken banh mi'; the correct identity is Bang Bang Chicken based on the description, aliases, cultural metadata, and nutritional profile.

confidence_reason: Nutrition values are estimated by adapting USDA FoodData Central data for cooked chicken breast and sauce components (sesame paste, peanut butter, soy sauce), as no exact approved-source entry for Bang Bang Chicken exists. The input values are consistent with this USDA-derived estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source entry exactly matches this composite dish.
notes: 1. IDENTITY CORRECTION: The input record carried the food name 'Chicken banh mi' (a Vietnamese-French sandwich), which is inconsistent with the description, aliases (棒棒雞/棒棒鸡), cultural metadata (Chinese/Sichuan), cooking style (poaching/pounding/cold mixing), and nutritional profile. The correct identity is Bang Bang Chicken (棒棒鸡), a classic Sichuan cold dish. The alias 'Chicken banh mi' has been retained in the aliases list to preserve the original input identifier. 2. SOURCE REPLACEMENT: The input source 'Sichuan University of Science and Engineering' is not on the Project Veritas approved source list and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central adapted estimates. 3. DIETARY TAGS CORRECTED: The input incorrectly listed 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' for a chicken dish; these have been removed. 4. SATURATED FAT ADDED: saturated_fat_g (2.2 g) estimated from USDA fatty acid profiles for cooked chicken and sesame/peanut components; not reported in input. 5. MONOUNSATURATED AND POLYUNSATURATED FAT ADDED: monounsaturated_fat_g (3.8 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.5 g) estimated from USDA profiles for chicken and nut-based sauce components. 6. CHOLESTEROL ADDED: cholesterol_mg (65.0 mg) estimated from USDA cooked chicken breast data. 7. WATER: water_g set to -1 as moisture data for this composite dish preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026. 8. TRANS FAT: trans_fat_g set to 0.0 as this dish uses natural animal and plant fats with negligible industrial trans fat content, consistent with USDA data for equivalent components. 9. PORTIONS DEDUPLICATED: Input contained two identical portion entries; deduplicated to one. 10. GI SOURCE: Input GI source 'Embedded GI Database' is not approved; replaced with University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate. 11. FOOD GROUP CORRECTED: Input listed 'Grains and staples / Bread-based dishes' which is incorrect for a chicken dish; corrected to 'Meat and alternatives / Poultry dishes'.
